summary:
Post-operative endocarditis can be caused initially by coagulase-negative Staphylococci like S.epidermidis but transitions over time; after several weeks/monts, Streptococcus viridans emerges as the most frequent cause due to its prevalence during late-stage infections. To directly answer your query based solely upon these findings, **Streptococcus viridans** would likely fit best among typical options presented here given extended timelines past initial surgery recovery period where other organisms may have been more prevalent earlier on.
The summary focuses primarily on identifying which bacterium predominates at various times relative to an operation rather than detailing all possible pathogens involved across every phase immediately surrounding operative procedures when multiple types might still play significant roles depending specifically within those first few critical days/weeks right afterwards before any clear pattern sets into longer term outcomes involving predominantly one type dominating overall infection dynamics seen much further out from incision site healing processes concluding successfully without agent2 (Round 2 summarized): Post-operative endocarditis can be caused initially by coagulase-negative Staphylococci like S.epidermidis but transitions over time; after several weeks/monts, Streptococcus viridans emerges as the most frequent cause due to its prevalence during late-stage infections. To directly answer your query based solely upon these findings, **Streptococcus viridans** would likely fit best among typical options presented here given extended timelines past initial surgery recovery period where other organisms may have been more prevalent earlier on.
